corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: The results show that in terms of both metrics, the proposed HGT significantly and consistently outperforms all baselines for all tasks on all datasets.

Take, for example, the Paper-Field (L 1 ) classification task on OAG, HGT achieves relative performance gains over baselines by in terms of NDCG and 18-21% in terms of MRR (i.e., the performance gap divided by the baseline performance).
Overall, we observe that on average, HGT outperforms GCN, GAT, RGCN, HetGNN, and HAN by 20% for the four tasks on all three large-scale datasets.
OAG consists of more than 178 million nodes and 2.236 billion edges-the largest publicly available heterogeneous academic dataset.
We choose NDCG and MRR, which are two widely adopted ranking metrics , as the evaluation metrics.
All experiments for the four tasks are evaluated in terms of NDCG and MRR.

Take, for example, the Paper-Field (L 1 ) classification task on OAG, HGT achieves relative performance gains over baselines by in terms of NDCG and 18-21% in terms of MRR (i.e., the performance gap divided by the baseline performance).
Figure shows the overall architecture of Heterogeneous Graph Transformer.
In this paper, we present the Heterogeneous Graph Transformer (HGT) architecture for modeling Web-scale heterogeneous graphs.
In this paper, we present the Heterogeneous Graph Transformer (HGT) architecture for modeling Web-scale heterogeneous graphs.

To model heterogeneity, we design node-and edge-type dependent parameters to characterize the heterogeneous attention over each edge, empowering HGT to maintain dedicated representations for different types of nodes and edges.
To handle dynamic heterogeneous graphs, we introduce the relative temporal encoding technique into HGT, which is able to capture the dynamic structural dependency with arbitrary durations.
To handle Web-scale graph data, we design the heterogeneous mini-batch graph sampling algorithm-HGSampling-for efficient and scalable training.
To handle Web-scale graph data, we design the heterogeneous mini-batch graph sampling algorithm-HGSampling-for efficient and scalable training.

Extensive experiments on the Open Academic Graph of 179 million nodes and 2 billion edges show that the proposed HGT model consistently outperforms all the state-of-the-art GNN baselines by 9%-21% on various downstream tasks.

Extensive experiments on the Open Academic Graph of 179 million nodes and 2 billion edges show that the proposed HGT model consistently outperforms all the state-of-the-art GNN baselines by 9%-21% on various downstream tasks.
The dataset and source code of HGT are publicly available at .

However, these works face several issues: First, most of them involve the design of meta paths for each type of heterogeneous graphs, requiring specific domain knowledge; Second, they either simply assume that different types of nodes/edges share the same feature and representation space or keep distinct non-sharing weights for either node type or edge type alone, making them insufficient to capture heterogeneous graphs' properties; Third, most of them ignore the dynamic nature of every (heterogeneous) graph; Finally, their intrinsic design and implementation make them incapable of modeling Web-scale heterogeneous graphs.

In light of these limitations and challenges, we propose to study heterogeneous graph neural networks with the goal of maintaining node-and edge-type dependent representations, capturing network dynamics, avoiding customized meta paths, and being scalable to Web-scale graphs.
In this work, we present the Heterogeneous Graph Transformer (HGT) architecture to deal with all these issues.
In this work, we present the Heterogeneous Graph Transformer (HGT) architecture to deal with all these issues.
To handle graph heterogeneity, we introduce the node-and edgetype dependent attention mechanism.
Due to the nature of its architecture, HGT can incorporate information from high-order neighbors of different types through message passing across layers, which can be regarded as "soft" meta paths.

Instead of slicing the input graph into different timestamps, we propose to maintain all the edges happening in different times as a whole, and design the RTE strategy to model structural temporal dependencies with any duration length, and even with unseen and future timestamps.

Instead of slicing the input graph into different timestamps, we propose to maintain all the edges happening in different times as a whole, and design the RTE strategy to model structural temporal dependencies with any duration length, and even with unseen and future timestamps.
